title: Making comfrey liquid fertiliser
subtitle: A potassium-rich liquid feed from a single perennial plant
excerpt: Comfrey liquid feed provides potassium, nitrogen, and phosphorus from a plant that accumulates nutrients from deep in the soil. A bucket of wilted leaves and three weeks produces enough feed for a full growing season.
sourceNotes: Lawrence Hills (HDRA founder) original comfrey research, republished in Garden Organic technical notes. RHS guidance on comfrey as a fertiliser plant. Organic Gardening Catalogue varietal and use notes on Symphytum x uplandicum Bocking 14.

content.text: Comfrey (Symphytum x uplandicum, variety Bocking 14) has a deep taproot. It draws up potassium, phosphorus, nitrogen, and trace minerals from depths that shallow-rooted crops cannot reach. The leaves break down quickly once cut and the nutrients release into solution within two to three weeks. The liquid is high in potassium and suits fruiting plants well: tomatoes, courgettes, beans, and soft fruit.
Potassium: roughly 3 percent dry weight. This is close to a diluted tomato feed.
Nitrogen: 0.7-1 percent dry weight. This is lower than grass clippings, but the liquid form goes straight to roots without further breakdown.
Phosphorus: 0.3-0.5 percent dry weight, released gradually.
Cut comfrey leaves and wilt them for 24-48 hours in a pile. Wilting breaks down the cells and speeds decomposition.
Pack the wilted leaves into a 10-litre bucket to about three-quarters full.
Fill with water. Cover with a loose lid or a piece of board; the decomposing leaves produce a strong smell.
Leave for two to three weeks in warm weather. Stir once or twice during this period.

Strain the liquid into a separate container and dilute 1:15 with water before applying to plants. The undiluted liquid is a brown-black colour and smells strongly of decomposition; at 1:15 dilution it should be the colour of weak tea.

Water the diluted feed onto the root zone, not onto leaves. Apply every two weeks from the point when fruiting plants are in active growth and starting to set fruit. Do not apply to leafy crops that are intended primarily for leaf harvest, as the high potassium can suppress leafy growth.

Bocking 14 is the non-seeding variety developed by HDRA specifically for garden use. Unlike wild comfrey it does not self-seed invasively. A single plant can be cut four to six times per growing season and will regrow from the crown within three to four weeks. Plant in a permanent position in partial shade where it will not be disturbed; the taproot can reach 1.8 m and the plant is difficult to remove once established.
Using leaves directly in the compost heap

Wilted comfrey leaves can also be added directly to a compost heap as a high-nutrient activator. A bucket of wilted leaves layered through a slow heap raises nitrogen and potassium simultaneously. The leaves break down within two to three weeks and are unrecognisable in the finished compost.
